properties.context: Master detective who solves the Adventure of the Blue Carbuncle; traces the jewel from goose crop to its thief James Ryder; releases Ryder on humanitarian grounds at Christmas,           Master detective who investigates the Five Orange Pips case; identifies the KKK as the threat and Captain Calhoun as the murderer; too late to save John Openshaw but sends five pips to Calhoun,          Master detective; investigated the Scandal in Bohemia case; disguised himself as a groom and later a clergyman; orchestrated the fire alarm at Briony Lodge; ultimately outwitted by Irene Adler,         Master detective who solves the Red-Headed League case; deduces Jabez Wilson is being used as a decoy to allow John Clay to tunnel into the City and Suburban Bank; orchestrates the vault ambush,        Master detective who solves the Adventure of the Speckled Band; deduces that Dr. Roylott used a swamp adder to murder Julia Stoner and planned the same for Helen; his cane blows drove the snake back through the ventilator onto Roylott,       Master detective who solves the Adventure of the Copper Beeches; deduces that Violet Hunter was hired to personate Alice Rucastle,        who was imprisoned; rescues Alice with Watson,      Master detective who solves the Adventure of the Beryl Coronet; deduces Arthur's innocence and identifies Sir George Burnwell and Mary Holder as the true culprits; recovers the three missing beryls,     Master detective who solves the Noble Bachelor case; deduces that Hatty Doran fled with her first husband Frank Moulton; locates them via hotel bill fragment; brings all parties together,    Master detective who solves the Man with the Twisted Lip case; disguised himself as an elderly opium addict in the Bar of Gold; deduced that Hugh Boone is Neville St. Clair in disguise; washed off the disguise in Bow Street cells,   Master detective who investigates the Boscombe Valley Mystery; deduces Turner's guilt and saves James McCarthy from wrongful conviction,  Master detective who investigates the Engineer's Thumb case; deduces the gang are counterfeiters using the hydraulic press; determines the location of the house by calculating the return trip distance, Master detective who solves the Case of Identity; deduces that Hosmer Angel is James Windibank in disguise and exposes him; references past gifts from the King of Bohemia (snuffbox) and reigning family of Holland (ring)
properties.chapter_id: chapter_7,           chapter_5,          chapter_1,         chapter_2,        chapter_8,       chapter_12,      chapter_11,     chapter_10,    chapter_6,   chapter_4,  chapter_9, chapter_3
properties.physical_description: tall,   spare figure,  thin knees,   hawk-like nose,  gaunt figure in long grey travelling-cloak and close-fitting cloth cap

properties.context: Narrator; visits Holmes on the second morning after Christmas; accompanies Holmes throughout the Blue Carbuncle investigation,           Narrator; staying at Baker Street while his wife visits her mother; accompanies Holmes during Five Orange Pips discussion,          Narrator; recently married; returned to civil medical practice; assisted Holmes in the fire alarm operation at Briony Lodge,         Narrator; accompanies Holmes throughout the Red-Headed League investigation including the bank vault ambush,        Narrator; shares rooms with Holmes at Baker Street; accompanies Holmes throughout the Speckled Band investigation; witnesses Turner's confession (reference to earlier chapter); assists in the night vigil at Stoke Moran,       Narrator; shares rooms with Holmes at Baker Street; accompanies Holmes to Winchester and the Copper Beeches,      Narrator; shares rooms with Holmes at Baker Street; accompanies Holmes to Fairbank in Streatham,     Narrator; staying with Holmes at Baker Street; reads the press cuttings to Holmes before Lord St. Simon's visit,    Narrator; went to the opium den to retrieve Isa Whitney and unexpectedly reunites with Holmes; accompanies Holmes to The Cedars and Bow Street,   Narrator; accompanies Holmes to investigate the Boscombe Valley Mystery; medical expertise helps analyse injuries; witnesses Turner's confession,  Narrator; treats Hatherley's thumb wound and introduces him to Holmes; accompanies Holmes to Eyford on the investigation, Narrator; shares rooms with Holmes at Baker Street; accompanies Holmes during the Case of Identity investigation
properties.chapter_id: chapter_7,           chapter_5,          chapter_1,         chapter_2,        chapter_8,       chapter_12,      chapter_11,     chapter_10,    chapter_6,   chapter_4,  chapter_9, chapter_3

properties.context: Commissionaire; found the hat and goose on Tottenham Court Road on Christmas morning; brought them to Holmes; his wife found the blue carbuncle in the goose's crop
Tallish man who dropped the goose and hat during a street altercation on Tottenham Court Road; member of the Alpha Inn goose club; innocent of any involvement with the carbuncle
properties.physical_description: large man, rounded shoulders, massive head, broad intelligent face, pointed grizzled brown beard, touch of red in nose and cheeks
properties.occupation: scholar / man of letters (fallen on hard times)

properties.context: Aristocratic owner of the blue carbuncle; staying at Hotel Cosmopolitan when the gem was stolen; offering £1000 reward
properties.title: Countess

properties.context: Plumber falsely accused of stealing the blue carbuncle; framed by James Ryder and Catherine Cusack; arrested and remanded to Assizes; innocent
Upper-attendant (head attendant) at Hotel Cosmopolitan; actual thief of the blue carbuncle; conspired with Catherine Cusack; hid the stone in a goose; released by Holmes on Christmas mercy
properties.physical_description: little rat-faced fellow; small; pale-cheeked

properties.context: Maid (waiting-maid) to the Countess of Morcar; confederate of James Ryder in the theft of the blue carbuncle; told Ryder about the stone
properties.occupation: maid / waiting-maid to Countess of Morcar

properties.context: Ryder's sister; lives at 117 Brixton Road; fattens fowls for market; supplied the geese including the one containing the carbuncle to Breckinridge
Salesman at Covent Garden Market; bought geese from Mrs. Oakshott and sold them to the Alpha Inn; irritable when questioned about the geese
properties.physical_description: horsey-looking man, sharp face, trim side-whiskers

properties.context: Landlord of the Alpha Inn; instituted the goose club; bought geese from Breckinridge and distributed them to club members
Ryder's criminal contact in Kilburn; had served time in Pentonville; Ryder intended to convert the stone to money through him
Referenced in newspaper account as the officer who arrested John Horner
Holmes's landlady; referenced humorously by Holmes in connection with examining the crop of a woodcock, Holmes's landlady at Baker Street; woken by Helen Stoner's early morning arrival
Goose club at the Alpha Inn near the British Museum; members paid weekly to receive a Christmas goose; Henry Baker was a member
Holmes's lodgings; location of initial deduction of hat; Henry Baker and James Ryder both brought here
Street where Peterson encountered Henry Baker being assaulted and found the goose and hat, Location of Mary Sutherland's father's plumbing business
Corner where the street altercation occurred that caused Henry Baker to drop his goose and hat
Hotel where the Countess of Morcar was staying when the blue carbuncle was stolen on December 22nd
Small public house at corner of a Holborn street in Bloomsbury, near the British Museum; Windigate's inn where Henry Baker was a goose club member
Market where Breckinridge had his stall; where Holmes and Watson find Breckinridge and encounter Ryder
Address of Mrs. Oakshott (Ryder's sister); where geese were reared; where Ryder hid the carbuncle in a goose
Area where Maudsley (Ryder's criminal contact) lived; Ryder intended to bring the stone-containing goose there
Location where the blue carbuncle was found

Precious blue gemstone found in the crop of Henry Baker's Christmas goose; property of the Countess of Morcar; stolen by Ryder with Cusack's help; found by Peterson's wife; valued far above the £1000 reward offered
properties.history: two murders, a vitriol-throwing, a suicide and several robberies

properties.context: Battered black hard-felt hat with initials H.B.; examined by Holmes to deduce Baker's circumstances; found with the goose by Peterson on Tottenham Court Road
The white goose with barred tail that contained the blue carbuncle in its crop; originally from Mrs. Oakshott via Breckinridge and the Alpha Inn goose club

properties.description: Theft of Blue Carbuncle from Hotel Cosmopolitan
properties.context: James Ryder, with Catherine Cusack's help, stole the blue carbuncle from the Countess of Morcar's jewel-case on December 22nd; John Horner was falsely accused

properties.description: Street Altercation on Tottenham Court Road
properties.context: Henry Baker was assaulted by roughs at Goodge Street corner on Christmas morning; dropped his goose and hat while fleeing; Peterson found both
Date when the blue carbuncle was stolen from Hotel Cosmopolitan

Watson's wife; on a visit to her mother during the events of the story,  Watson's wife; old school friend of Kate Whitney; encouraged Watson to help retrieve Isa Whitney, Watson's wife; encourages Watson to go with Holmes to Boscombe Valley
Young client of Holmes; aged about 22; came from Horsham; received five orange pips from the KKK; murdered the same night he visited Holmes near Waterloo Bridge

John's uncle; Confederate veteran; planter in Florida; returned to England ~1869; received first KKK letter from Pondicherry in March 1883; burned KKK papers; found drowned in garden pool May 2nd 1883

John Openshaw's father; owner of bicycle tyre factory in Coventry; inherited Horsham estate from Elias; received second KKK letter from Dundee in January 1885; died in chalk-pit fall near Portsdown Hill
properties.status: deceased (murdered by KKK, verdict: accidental death)
properties.occupation: factory owner / patentee of Openshaw unbreakable tyre

properties.context: Leader of the KKK gang aboard the barque Lone Star; identified by Holmes as Openshaw's murderer; one of three native-born Americans on the Lone Star; sent five orange pips by Holmes
Horsham lawyer summoned by Colonel Elias Openshaw to witness his will after receiving the KKK letter
Referenced as having recommended Holmes to John Openshaw; Holmes had saved him in the Tankerville Club scandal
Old friend of Joseph Openshaw; commands one of the forts on Portsdown Hill; telegraphed John Openshaw about his father's accident
H Division constable on duty near Waterloo Bridge who heard a cry for help and a splash the night Openshaw was murdered
Referenced in Colonel Openshaw's diary page from March 1869: 'Hudson came. Same old platform.' Likely a KKK contact
Referenced in Colonel Openshaw's diary; sent orange pips on 7th March 1869; 'cleared' (fled) by 9th March
Referenced in Colonel Openshaw's diary; sent orange pips on 7th March 1869; 'visited' on 12th March with a sinister implication
Referenced in Colonel Openshaw's diary; sent orange pips on 7th March 1869; 'cleared' by 10th March
Secret organisation formed by ex-Confederate soldiers after the American Civil War; terrorised negro voters and opponents; sent orange pips as warning; responsible for deaths of the Openshaw family

Holmes and Watson's shared rooms; location of initial meeting with John Openshaw,        Holmes's lodgings; where Watson visited Holmes and where the King of Bohemia called,       Holmes's residence; location of initial meeting with Jabez Wilson and post-case debrief,      Holmes and Watson's shared lodgings at time of this case (early in their association),     Holmes and Watson's shared rooms; where the initial letter from Violet Hunter was received and where they discussed the case,    Holmes and Watson's shared rooms; where Holder came to consult Holmes,   Holmes and Watson's shared rooms; location of initial meetings with Lord St. Simon and Lestrade; location of the final supper scene,  Holmes's lodgings; where Watson takes Hatherley after treating him, Holmes's lodgings; location of initial meetings with Mary Sutherland and later with James Windibank
properties.chapter_id: chapter_5,        chapter_1,       chapter_2,      chapter_8,     chapter_12,    chapter_11,   chapter_10,  chapter_9, chapter_3

properties.context: Town in Sussex near Colonel Openshaw's estate and later John Openshaw's residence
American state where Colonel Elias Openshaw had his plantation
City where Joseph Openshaw had his bicycle tyre factory
City in India from which the first KKK warning letter was sent to Colonel Openshaw in March 1883
Scottish city from which the second KKK warning letter was sent to Joseph Openshaw in January 1885
Home port of the barque Lone Star; where Holmes addressed his warning envelope to Captain Calhoun
London location near where John Openshaw was murdered; his body was recovered from the Thames nearby
Railway station from which Openshaw was planning to take the last train back to Horsham the night he was murdered
London dock where Holmes located the Lone Star before she departed down the river
Location of forts commanded by Major Freebody; where Joseph Openshaw was visiting when he fell into the chalk-pit
Found face-down in garden pool; verdict: suicide; suspected KKK murder; occurred on the night of May 2nd 1883, seven weeks after receiving first KKK letter
Fell over chalk-pit near Portsdown Hill; verdict: accidental death; suspected KKK murder; January 1885, three days after receiving KKK letter
Pushed into the Thames near Waterloo Bridge on the night he visited Holmes; reported as accidental drowning
The barque Lone Star was destroyed in severe equinoctial gales in the Atlantic; only a shattered stern-post with 'L.S.' was ever found
KKK warning sent in an envelope before each murder; received by Colonel Openshaw from Pondicherry (March 1883), Joseph Openshaw from Dundee (January 1885), and John Openshaw from East London
Small brass cashbox belonging to Colonel Openshaw; labelled with KKK initials; contained papers (letters, memoranda, receipts, register) which were burned by the Colonel before his death
Single sheet of blue-tinted paper escaped from the burning; dated March 1869; contains entries about sending pips to McCauley, Paramore and John Swain; presented to Holmes by John Openshaw
American sailing ship captained by James Calhoun; present at Pondicherry (Jan-Feb 1883), Dundee (January 1885), and London; used by KKK operatives; destroyed in Atlantic gales
Envelope containing five orange pips addressed to Captain James Calhoun, Barque Lone Star, Savannah, Georgia; inscribed 'S.H. for J.O.' inside flap
Date on which Colonel Elias Openshaw received the first KKK warning letter from Pondicherry
Night on which Colonel Elias Openshaw died

Well-known adventuress; born in New Jersey 1858; contralto singer; former prima donna at Imperial Opera Warsaw; the only woman who outwitted Holmes; secretly married Godfrey Norton; fled to the Continent; kept the compromising photograph for self-protection
properties.status: married to Godfrey Norton; fled to Continent

properties.context: Grand Duke of Cassel-Felstein; hereditary King of Bohemia; visited Holmes disguised as Count Von Kramm; had past romantic entanglement with Irene Adler; sought to recover a compromising photograph before his betrothal to Clotilde Lothman von Saxe-Meningen
properties.title: King of Bohemia / Grand Duke of Cassel-Felstein
properties.physical_description: over six feet six, chest and limbs of a Hercules, black vizard mask, barbaric opulence

properties.context: Lawyer at the Inner Temple; frequent visitor to Irene Adler; married her at the Church of St. Monica; drove back to the Temple after the wedding; fled with Irene to the Continent
properties.physical_description: dark, aquiline, moustached, remarkably handsome
properties.status: married to Irene Adler; fled to Continent

properties.context: Second daughter of the King of Scandinavia; betrothed to the King of Bohemia; known for strict principles and delicacy of character
properties.title: Princess

properties.context: Father of Clotilde Lothman von Saxe-Meningen; known for strict family principles
Watson's careless servant girl; given notice by Watson's wife
Holmes's landlady at Baker Street; brought in a food tray for Holmes and Watson
Hereditary kings of Bohemia; implicated in the scandal involving Irene Adler
Irene Adler's residence at Serpentine Avenue, St. John's Wood; bijou villa; where Holmes staged the fire alarm; where the compromising photograph was hidden
Church on Edgeware Road where Holmes inadvertently served as witness to the marriage of Irene Adler and Godfrey Norton
Kingdom ruled by the King; origin of the letter paper (Egria); involved in the scandal
City from which the King of Bohemia travelled incognito to consult Holmes
City where the King of Bohemia made the acquaintance of Irene Adler; also where she was prima donna at the Imperial Opera
Hotel where the King of Bohemia stayed under alias Count Von Kramm
Station from which Irene Adler and Godfrey Norton departed for the Continent by the 5:15 train
Town in Bohemia where the letter paper was manufactured; near Carlsbad; scene of Wallenstein's death
Legal institution where Godfrey Norton practised as a lawyer

properties.description: Marriage of Irene Adler and Godfrey Norton
properties.context: Surprise marriage at Church of St. Monica; Holmes inadvertently served as legal witness when dragged to the altar
properties.date: Day before Holmes's fire alarm operation
properties.location: Church of St. Monica, Edgeware Road

properties.description: Fire Alarm Operation at Briony Lodge
properties.context: Holmes staged a fire alarm using a smoke-rocket to trick Irene Adler into revealing the photograph's location
Undated unsigned letter written on Bohemian paper from Egria, announcing a masked visitor; analysed by Holmes to identify its German/Bohemian origin
Photograph of both Irene Adler and the King of Bohemia together; hidden behind a sliding panel above the right bell-pull at Briony Lodge; central object of the case; kept by Adler for self-protection

properties.description: Letter from Irene Norton née Adler to Holmes
properties.context: Found at Briony Lodge; revealed Adler's awareness of Holmes's plan; announced her flight; signed Irene Norton née Adler; dated midnight; left alongside a photograph of Adler in evening dress
Plumber's smoke-rocket used by Watson to create a false fire alarm at Briony Lodge on Holmes's instruction
Ring offered by the King of Bohemia to Holmes as reward; Holmes declined in favour of Irene Adler's photograph
Night Watson returned through Baker Street and desired to see Holmes
Stout red-headed pawnbroker from Saxe-Coburg Square; client of Holmes; victim of the Red-Headed League scheme used to keep him away from his shop while John Clay tunnelled into the adjacent bank
properties.physical_description: very stout, florid-faced, elderly, fiery red hair, baggy grey trousers, black frock-coat
properties.history: former ship's carpenter; visited China; Freemason

properties.context: Wilson's pawnbroker assistant; actually John Clay in disguise; mastermind behind the Red-Headed League tunnel scheme to rob the City and Suburban Bank
properties.physical_description: small, stout-built, quick, no facial hair, under 30, white acid splash on forehead, pierced ears, clean-cut boyish face
properties.occupation: pawnbroker's assistant (cover); murderer, thief, smasher, forger
properties.background: grandson of a royal duke; educated at Eton and Oxford

properties.context: John Clay's accomplice with a shock of very red hair; escaped down the hole initially but was caught by police at the door
properties.physical_description: lithe and small, pale face, shock of very red hair

properties.context: Manager of the fraudulent Red-Headed League office at Pope's Court; alias used by Archie/Clay's associate; also known as William Morris
properties.physical_description: small man with extremely red hair

properties.context: Official police agent from Scotland Yard; assists Holmes in the bank vault ambush; had been pursuing John Clay for years
properties.physical_description: bulky
traits: limited in professional skill per Holmes

properties.context: Chairman of directors of the City and Suburban Bank; accompanied Holmes and Watson in the vault ambush to protect the French gold
properties.physical_description: long, thin, sad-faced man, very shiny hat, oppressively respectable frock-coat

properties.context: Fictional deceased American millionaire from Lebanon, Pennsylvania; supposedly the founder and benefactor of the Red-Headed League; invented by John Clay as part of the scheme
properties.physical_description: red-headed

properties.context: Famous musician/violinist performing at St. James's Hall; Holmes and Watson attended his concert during the investigation
French author quoted by Holmes at the end of the chapter: 'L'homme c'est rien—l'oeuvre c'est tout'
Recipient of the Flaubert letter referenced by Holmes at the end of the chapter
Fraudulent organisation created by John Clay and Duncan Ross to lure Jabez Wilson away from his pawnshop during tunnel excavation; dissolved October 9, 1890
Bank adjacent to Saxe-Coburg Square; targeted by John Clay's tunnel robbery scheme; holds 30,000 napoleons of French gold borrowed from Bank of France

Official police organisation; Peter Jones is its agent; Holmes contacted them after the concert to arrange the vault ambush,   Official police force investigating the disappearance of Lady St. Simon; Lestrade represents them,  Official police organisation; Lestrade is its detective on the Boscombe Valley case, Official police; had been tracking the counterfeiting gang as far as Reading; Inspector Bradstreet represents them in this investigation
Institution from which the City and Suburban Bank borrowed 30,000 napoleons
Organisation to which Jabez Wilson belongs; identified by Holmes from Wilson's arc-and-compass breastpin
Location of Jabez Wilson's pawnbroker business; poky, shabby-genteel small square near the City; back of premises used for tunnel excavation
Address of the Red-Headed League's fraudulent office at number 7
Origin of the fictional benefactor Ezekiah Hopkins
Concert venue where Holmes and Watson attended Sarasate's performance during the investigation
Underground station where Holmes and Watson alighted on their way to Saxe-Coburg Square
Neighbourhood where Watson's home is located
False address given for William Morris's new offices; turned out to be a manufacturer of artificial knee-caps
properties.address: 17 King Edward Street, near St. Paul's

properties.context: Underground cellar of the City and Suburban Bank targeted by John Clay's tunnel; site of the arrest
Fraudulent advertisement placed in The Morning Chronicle on April 27, 1890, offering £4 a week to red-headed men to copy the Encyclopædia Britannica
Announced by a cardboard notice on the door of 7 Pope's Court on October 9, 1890, signalling the tunnel was complete
Holmes, Watson, Peter Jones and Merryweather waited in the City and Suburban Bank vault to catch John Clay and Archie attempting the robbery
John Clay (as Vincent Spaulding) spent months digging a tunnel from Wilson's pawnshop cellar to the City and Suburban Bank vault
Bullion borrowed from the Bank of France by the City and Suburban Bank; target of John Clay's tunnel robbery
Watson's revolver brought on Holmes's instruction and placed cocked during the vault ambush
Small white cardboard nailed to the door of 7 Pope's Court announcing 'THE RED-HEADED LEAGUE IS DISSOLVED. October 9, 1890.'
Wilson was tasked with copying the Encyclopædia Britannica as nominal duty in the Red-Headed League scheme
Newspaper in which the Red-Headed League advertisement was published on April 27, 1890
Date of The Morning Chronicle containing the Red-Headed League advertisement
Date of dissolution of the Red-Headed League, announced on a cardboard notice

Client of Holmes; twin sister of the deceased Julia Stoner; stepdaughter of Dr. Roylott; engaged to Percy Armitage; moved into Julia's room due to repairs, heard the whistle, and sought Holmes's help before she too could be murdered
properties.physical_description: woman of thirty, hair shot with premature grey, features drawn and grey, restless frightened eyes

properties.context: Last survivor of the Roylotts of Stoke Moran; stepfather of Helen and Julia Stoner; murderer of Julia using a swamp adder; planned to kill Helen too; killed by his own snake after Holmes drove it back through the ventilator
properties.physical_description: huge, extremely tall, large face seared with wrinkles, burned yellow, deep-set bile-shot eyes, high thin fleshless nose

properties.context: Twin sister of Helen; stepdaughter of Dr. Roylott; murdered by the swamp adder approximately two years before the story; her dying words 'It was the band! The speckled band!' provided the key clue; was engaged to a half-pay major of marines at the time of her death
properties.physical_description: hair beginning to whiten prematurely

properties.context: Helen Stoner's fiancé; second son of Mr. Armitage of Crane Water near Reading
properties.background: second son of Mr. Armitage of Crane Water

properties.description: Mrs. Stoner (deceased mother of Helen and Julia)
properties.context: Young widow of Major-General Stoner; married Dr. Roylott in India; had considerable sum of money (~£1100/year) bequeathed to Roylott; died eight years ago in railway accident near Crewe
Bengal Artillery; first husband of Mrs. Stoner; father of Helen and Julia
Aunt of Helen and Julia Stoner; mother's maiden sister; lives near Harrow; Helen taken to her after Roylott's death
Person helped by Holmes in connection with an opal tiara; referred Helen Stoner to Holmes
Half-pay major of marines to whom Julia Stoner became engaged at Miss Westphail's at Christmas; the impending marriage was the trigger for Roylott's murder plan
Well-known Surrey family; one of the oldest Saxon families in England; estates once extended into Berkshire and Hampshire; ruined by dissolute heirs and a gambler in the Regency
Ancient manor house of the Roylotts on the western border of Surrey; two-hundred-year-old house; three ground-floor bedrooms in inhabited wing; scene of Julia Stoner's murder and the night vigil
properties.address: Stoke Moran, western border of Surrey

properties.context: Railway station near Stoke Moran; Helen Stoner arrived here by dog-cart; Holmes and Watson hired a trap here
Village inn opposite Stoke Moran; where Holmes and Watson stayed during their investigation; from which they could see the manor windows
Indian city where Dr. Roylott established a large medical practice; where he married Mrs. Stoner; where he beat his native butler to death
Location near where Miss Westphail lives; Helen taken there after Roylott's death
Location near where Mrs. Stoner was killed in a railway accident eight years before the story
Location where Holmes went to consult the will of the deceased Mrs. Stoner to determine financial motive
Julia was killed by a swamp adder sent through the ventilator by Dr. Roylott approximately two weeks before her wedding; her dying words were 'It was the band! The speckled band!'
properties.date: approximately two years before the story; about a fortnight before Julia's planned wedding
properties.cause_of_death: swamp adder bite (disguised as unknown cause)

properties.context: Dr. Roylott was killed by his own swamp adder after Holmes's cane blows drove the snake back through the ventilator; died within ten seconds of being bitten; official verdict: met his fate while indiscreetly playing with a dangerous pet
properties.verdict: accidental death while playing with dangerous pet

properties.context: Deadliest snake in India; used by Dr. Roylott to murder Julia Stoner; trained to return through the ventilator when summoned by whistle; driven back by Holmes's cane and bit Roylott fatally
properties.physical_description: yellow band with brownish speckles, squat diamond-shaped head, puffed neck

properties.context: Fake bell-pull in Julia's room, fastened to a hook above the ventilator; used as a bridge for the snake to climb down from the ventilator to the bed

properties.description: Ventilator (between Roylott's room and Julia's room)
properties.context: Small opening between Dr. Roylott's room and the adjacent bedroom; used to pass the snake through; installed around the same time as the dummy bell-rope
Large iron safe in Dr. Roylott's bedroom; used to store and transport the swamp adder; metallic clang heard by Helen when Roylott hastily closed it after recalling the snake

properties.description: Dog-whip / Dog Lash with Whipcord Loop
properties.context: Small dog lash found hung on corner of Roylott's bed; tied in a loop of whipcord; used by Roylott to handle the snake; used by Holmes to lasso and remove the snake after Roylott's death
Found on top of Roylott's iron safe; used to train and recall the swamp adder
Consulted by Holmes at Doctors' Commons; showed financial motive for Roylott to prevent stepdaughters' marriages; total income ~£750 at time of case; each daughter could claim £250 on marriage

Client of Holmes; governess hired by Jephro Rucastle; instructed to cut her hair, wear a blue dress and sit at a window to impersonate Alice Rucastle; discovered Alice's imprisonment; sent the telegram to Holmes; became head of a private school at Walsall after the case
properties.physical_description: bright quick face, freckled, luxuriant chestnut hair (cut short), brisk manner
properties.occupation: governess; later head of private school at Walsall

properties.context: Employer of Violet Hunter; owner of the Copper Beeches; imprisoned his daughter Alice to control her inheritance; used Hunter to impersonate Alice; severely mauled by his own mastiff Carlo when he returned to confront Holmes; survived but remained a broken man
properties.physical_description: prodigiously stout, very smiling face, great heavy chin, small eyes, age approximately 45

properties.context: Second wife of Jephro Rucastle; silent, pale-faced, much younger than her husband; passionately devoted to him and their son; appeared to have a secret sorrow; remained devoted to Rucastle after the attack
properties.physical_description: silent, pale-faced, light grey eyes, under 30, much younger than Rucastle

properties.context: Rucastle's daughter from first marriage; had been told to have gone to Philadelphia; was actually imprisoned in the locked wing of the Copper Beeches; Rucastle wanted control of her inheritance; had brain-fever and hair cut off during illness; her fiancé Mr. Fowler rescued her; married Fowler by special licence in Southampton

Alice Rucastle's fiancé; stood outside the Copper Beeches watching for Alice; the man seen in the road by Violet Hunter; bribed Toller to drink and left a ladder; rescued Alice through the skylight; married Alice by special licence in Southampton; now holds a government appointment in Mauritius
properties.physical_description: small bearded man in a grey suit

properties.context: Groom/servant at Copper Beeches; rough uncouth man; only person who could handle Carlo the mastiff; frequently drunk; bribed by Fowler to be incapacitated; inadvertently left key in forbidden door; when sobered, alerted Holmes and Watson when Carlo attacked Rucastle
properties.physical_description: rough, uncouth, grizzled hair and whiskers, perpetual smell of drink

properties.context: Toller's wife; servant at Copper Beeches; tall strong woman; locked in cellar by Violet Hunter; revealed the full story to Holmes; was Alice's friend; helped facilitate Fowler's plan
properties.physical_description: very tall and strong woman, sour face, silent

properties.context: Young son of Jephro and Mrs. Rucastle; six years old; abnormally cruel disposition; given to sadistic behaviour with animals and insects
properties.physical_description: small for age, head disproportionately large

properties.context: Rucastle's giant mastiff; kept half-starved; let loose at night by Toller; used to prevent anyone approaching the house at night or communicating with Alice; attacked and severely mauled Rucastle; shot dead by Watson
properties.physical_description: giant dog as large as a calf, tawny, hanging jowl, black muzzle, huge projecting bones

properties.context: Manager of Westaway's governess agency in the West End; showed annoyance when Violet Hunter refused the Rucastle position as it cost her a commission
Previous employer of Violet Hunter; received appointment in Halifax, Nova Scotia and took his children to America, leaving Hunter without a position
Well-known agency for governesses in the West End; managed by Miss Stoper; where Violet Hunter went weekly looking for work

Rucastle's large whitewashed country house near Winchester; scene of Alice's imprisonment; named after a clump of copper beech trees in front of the door; woods on three sides part of Lord Southerton's preserves
properties.address: near Winchester, Hampshire (five miles from Winchester on far side)

properties.context: Town near the Copper Beeches; where Holmes and Watson met Violet Hunter at the Black Swan Hotel
Inn in the High Street of Winchester where Holmes and Watson met Violet Hunter for the midday meeting
Where Alice Rucastle was said to have gone (cover story); she was actually imprisoned at the Copper Beeches
Town where Fowler and Alice Rucastle married by special licence the day after their escape
Island where Mr. Fowler now holds a government appointment after marrying Alice Rucastle
Town where Violet Hunter became head of a private school after the case
Uninhabited wing of the Copper Beeches where Alice Rucastle was imprisoned; a centre door barricaded with an iron bar and padlocked; a skylight through which Fowler rescued Alice

Jephro Rucastle imprisoned his daughter Alice to control her inheritance and prevent her marriage to Mr. Fowler; Alice had brain-fever during which her hair was cut; hired Violet Hunter to impersonate her to mislead Fowler
Mr. Fowler, aided by Mrs. Toller, used a ladder through the skylight to remove Alice from the locked wing while the Rucastles were out; Holmes and Watson arrived to find the room empty
Carlo the mastiff attacked and severely mauled Rucastle when he returned to the house; Watson shot and killed the dog; Rucastle survived but was permanently broken
Dress belonging to Alice Rucastle that Violet Hunter was made to wear while sitting at the window to impersonate Alice for Fowler's benefit
Coil of chestnut hair matching Violet Hunter's hair found by Hunter in a locked drawer; was Alice Rucastle's hair cut off during her brain-fever illness

Senior partner of Holder & Stevenson banking firm; client of Holmes; accepted the Beryl Coronet as security for a £50,000 loan; wrongly accused his son Arthur; eventually reconciled with Arthur after Holmes revealed the truth
properties.occupation: banker; senior partner of Holder & Stevenson
properties.physical_description: tall, portly, imposing, massive strongly marked face

properties.context: Alexander's only son; discovered Mary Holder stealing the coronet; wrestled Sir George Burnwell to recover it; lost three gems in the struggle but kept silent to protect Mary; wrongly arrested; freed after Holmes revealed the truth
properties.character: wild, wayward, heavy gambler; but chivalrous

properties.context: Alexander's niece and adopted daughter; secretly involved with Sir George Burnwell; passed the coronet out of the window to him; fled with Burnwell after writing a farewell note to her uncle
properties.physical_description: above middle height, slim, dark hair and eyes, deadly pale skin

properties.context: Villain; Arthur's friend; romantic influence over Mary Holder; received the coronet through the window; struggled with Arthur; sold the three gems to a receiver for £600; confronted by Holmes who recovered the gems for £1000 each
properties.physical_description: older than Arthur, man of the world, great personal beauty
properties.character: ruined gambler, desperate villain, man without heart or conscience

properties.context: Second waiting-maid at Fairbank; had been in service only a few months; had a sweetheart (Francis Prosper) she secretly went out to see; may have overheard Alexander Holder discussing the coronet
Greengrocer who delivers vegetables to Fairbank; sweetheart of Lucy Parr; has a wooden leg; his wooden-leg footprints in the snow were identified by Holmes
properties.physical_description: has a wooden leg

properties.context: A person of the highest nobility in England who borrowed £50,000 from Holder & Stevenson using the Beryl Coronet as security; identity deliberately withheld
Second largest private banking concern in the City of London; Alexander Holder is senior partner; located on Threadneedle Street
Alexander Holder's modest residence in Streatham; good-sized square house of white stone; scene of the coronet theft
Location of Holder & Stevenson banking firm, City street where Hugh Boone had his regular begging spot on the left-hand side
Lane running beside Fairbank leading to the stables; where Sir George Burnwell waited; where Arthur chased and struggled with Burnwell; where key footprints in the snow were found by Holmes

Mary Holder stole the coronet from Alexander's bureau, passed it through the hall window to Sir George Burnwell; Arthur pursued Burnwell and recovered most of the coronet but three gems were lost in the struggle
properties.date: night before Holder came to Holmes

properties.context: One of the most precious public possessions of the Empire; contains 39 enormous beryls in gold; used as security for a £50,000 loan; three beryls torn off in the struggle between Arthur and Sir George Burnwell
Three beryls torn from the coronet during the struggle between Arthur and Sir George Burnwell; sold by Burnwell to a receiver for £600; recovered by Holmes for £3,000 (£1,000 each)
Noble client; second son of the Duke of Balmoral; married Hatty Doran who subsequently disappeared at the wedding breakfast; consulted Holmes; refused to stay for supper after the truth was revealed
properties.full_name: Lord Robert Walsingham de Vere St. Simon
properties.title: Lord
properties.physical_description: pleasant cultured face, high-nosed, pale, slight forward stoop, grizzled hair, careful foppish dress

properties.description: Hatty Doran / Lady St. Simon / Mrs. Francis Hay Moulton
properties.context: Only daughter of Aloysius Doran; secretly married to Frank Moulton before her marriage to Lord St. Simon; fled wedding breakfast upon recognising the living Moulton in church pew; now known as Mrs. Francis Hay Moulton
properties.alias: Lady St. Simon / Mrs. Francis Hay Moulton
properties.physical_description: lustrous black hair, large dark eyes, exquisite mouth; graceful figure and striking face

properties.context: Hatty Doran's first and true husband; believed dead after Apache attack in Montana; tracked Hatty to London; appeared in church pew at her second wedding; reunited with her; living at 226 Gordon Square
properties.physical_description: small, wiry, sunburnt man, clean-shaven, sharp face, alert manner

properties.context: Father of Hatty Doran; richest man on the Pacific slope; made fortune in gold mining in California; brought Hatty to London; hosted wedding breakfast at Lancaster Gate
properties.residence: Lancaster Gate, London (furnished house taken for the season)

properties.context: Former dancer at the Allegro; had a close relationship with Lord St. Simon for several years; caused a disturbance at the wedding breakfast; arrested by police; walked with Hatty Doran in Hyde Park; note with initials 'F.H.M.' in bride's dress pocket (misread as Flora's by Lestrade)

Scotland Yard detective working the St. Simon case; dragged the Serpentine looking for Lady St. Simon's body; misread the initials 'F.H.M.' as Flora's; visited Baker Street with recovered wedding clothes
Father of Lord St. Simon; former Secretary for Foreign Affairs; had been compelled to sell his pictures in recent years
properties.title: Duke

properties.context: Referred Lord St. Simon to Holmes; present at the wedding; Holmes and Hatty were to honeymoon at his place near Petersfield
properties.title: Lord

properties.context: Present at the wedding at St. George's, Hanover Square
properties.title: Duchess

properties.context: Younger brother of Lord St. Simon; present at the wedding
properties.title: Lord

properties.context: Sister of Lord St. Simon; present at the wedding
properties.title: Lady

properties.context: Wedding guest present at St. George's, Hanover Square
properties.title: Lady

properties.context: American maid who came from California with Hatty Doran; confidential servant; knew Frank Moulton in California; told where Hatty had gone when her father came looking
Church where the wedding of Lord St. Simon and Hatty Doran took place; where Frank Moulton appeared in a pew
Address of Mr. Aloysius Doran's furnished house in London; location of the wedding breakfast
Address in London where Frank and Hatty Moulton took lodgings; where Holmes found them
Hatty Doran was seen walking into Hyde Park after leaving the breakfast; Lestrade dragged the Serpentine looking for her body; wedding clothes found floating near the Serpentine margin
City where Lord St. Simon first met Hatty Doran; where Aloysius Doran took Hatty after separating her from Frank Moulton
Mining camp where Frank Moulton and Hatty Doran first met and became engaged in 1884
Residence of Lord St. Simon; from where he wrote his letter to Holmes
Hotel in London where Francis H. Moulton had stayed; Holmes found their forwarding address (Gordon Square) here from the hotel register
Location of Lord Backwater's place where the honeymoon was to be spent

properties.description: Wedding of Lord St. Simon and Hatty Doran
properties.context: Held at St. George's, Hanover Square; Hatty dropped her bouquet into Frank Moulton's pew and received a note; she subsequently fled the wedding breakfast after seeing Frank through the window
Hatty left the wedding breakfast, changed into an ulster and bonnet, was seen walking with Flora Millar into Hyde Park, and went to join Frank Moulton at Gordon Square

properties.description: First Marriage of Hatty Doran and Frank Moulton
properties.context: Secret marriage in San Francisco in 1884 with a clergyman in waiting; kept secret from Aloysius Doran; Moulton then left to make his fortune

properties.description: Wedding Clothes and Ring (found in Serpentine)
properties.context: Hatty's wedding-dress of watered silk, white satin shoes, bride's wreath and veil, and new wedding ring; found floating near margin of Serpentine by a park-keeper; thrown there by Frank Moulton to destroy traces

Fragment of hotel bill from Northumberland Avenue hotel found in pocket of wedding dress; note on other side signed 'F.H.M.' (Frank Hay Moulton); misread by Lestrade as Flora H. Millar; used by Holmes to trace Moulton to Gordon Square
Brother of the late Elias Whitney; opium addict; found by Watson in the Bar of Gold; sent home in a cab
properties.physical_description: yellow pasty face, drooping lids, pin-point pupils

properties.context: Wife of Isa Whitney; old school friend of Mrs. Watson; came to Watson for help retrieving her husband from the opium den
Late brother of Isa Whitney; was D.D., Principal of the Theological College of St. George's
properties.title: D.D.
properties.occupation: Principal of Theological College of St. George's

properties.context: Wealthy resident of Lee, Kent; secretly led a double life as the beggar Hugh Boone; disappeared from the opium den; revealed to be Hugh Boone in disguise when Holmes wipes off his makeup in the Bow Street cell
properties.physical_description: pale, sad-faced, refined-looking, black-haired, smooth-skinned

properties.context: Alias of Neville St. Clair; professional beggar with hideous appearance; lodger at opium den; arrested for suspected murder of Neville St. Clair
properties.physical_description: shock of orange hair, horrible scar twisting upper lip, bulldog chin, penetrating dark eyes, cripple (limp)
properties.occupation: professional beggar (pretending to sell wax vestas)

properties.context: Neville St. Clair's wife; daughter of a local brewer; witnessed her husband at the second-floor window of the opium den; received a letter from her husband posted from Gravesend
properties.physical_description: little blonde woman, light mousseline de soie dress, fluffy pink chiffon

properties.context: Sallow Malay manager/proprietor of the Bar of Gold opium den; accessory to Neville St. Clair's concealment; passed St. Clair's letter and ring to a sailor customer
properties.physical_description: sallow Malay

properties.context: Dane who acts as assistant at the Bar of Gold opium den; helped the Lascar push Mrs. St. Clair out of the building
Inspector who had charge of the Neville St. Clair disappearance case; made a careful examination of the opium den premises

Tall stout police inspector at Bow Street; on duty when Holmes arrives to reveal Hugh Boone's true identity; agreed to hush up the matter, Scotland Yard inspector who accompanies Holmes,  Watson and Hatherley to Eyford; had been tracking the counterfeiting gang as far as Reading
properties.affiliation: Bow Street Police Station, Scotland Yard
properties.physical_description: tall, stout, peaked cap and frogged jacket

properties.context: Institution where Elias Whitney was Principal
Shipping company with offices in Fresno Street; Mrs. St. Clair received a telegram to collect a parcel from them, bringing her to Upper Swandam Lane
Bank where Neville St. Clair had £220 in credit
Opium den in Upper Swandam Lane; run by the Lascar; where Isa Whitney was found; where Neville St. Clair maintained his Hugh Boone disguise
Vile alley lurking behind high wharves north of the river east of London Bridge; location of Bar of Gold opium den
Neville St. Clair's large villa near Lee in Kent; where Holmes stays while conducting the inquiry; where Mrs. St. Clair awaits news
Village in Kent where Neville St. Clair lived; seven miles from Upper Swandam Lane
Police station where Hugh Boone (Neville St. Clair) was held; where Holmes reveals St. Clair's true identity
Street branching off Upper Swandam Lane; where the Aberdeen Shipping Company offices were located; where Mrs. St. Clair met the police inspector
Town from which Neville St. Clair's letter to his wife was posted (via the Lascar's sailor contact)
Town where Neville St. Clair's father was a schoolmaster and where St. Clair received his education

On the Monday before the story's events, Neville St. Clair was seen by his wife at the window of the Bar of Gold opium den and subsequently vanished; his coat was found stuffed with coins in the river; he was actually arrested as Hugh Boone
Holmes uses a wet sponge to wash off Hugh Boone's makeup in his Bow Street cell, revealing him to be Neville St. Clair
St. Clair's coat stuffed with 421 pennies and 270 half-pennies and thrown into the river to make it sink; found on the mud-bank when the tide receded

properties.description: Letter from Neville St. Clair to Mrs. St. Clair
properties.context: Written in pencil on the flyleaf of a book; posted from Gravesend; saying 'Dearest do not be frightened. All will come well. There is a huge error which it may take some little time to rectify. Wait in patience.—NEVILLE'
Neville St. Clair's signet ring; enclosed with his letter to Mrs. St. Clair; slipped off by St. Clair and given to the Lascar to pass on
Neville St. Clair's begging disguise; included a flesh-coloured plaster to twist lip, red wig, and face paint/grime; removed by Holmes with a wet sponge
The night Watson finds Isa Whitney in the opium den and encounters Holmes
Australian ex-pat; tenant farmer at Hatherley Farm; murdered at Boscombe Pool by John Turner; former highway robber who blackmailed Turner for 20 years; victim
Son of Charles McCarthy; arrested for his father's murder; innocent; in love with Alice Turner; secretly married a Bristol barmaid; acquitted at Assizes
properties.physical_description: comely to look at

properties.context: Wealthy landowner who made his money at Victorian gold mines; former highway robber known as 'Black Jack of Ballarat'; murdered Charles McCarthy who had been blackmailing him; dying of diabetes; confessed to Holmes
properties.status: dying of diabetes; died 7 months after confession
properties.physical_description: slow limping step, bowed shoulders, hard deep-lined craggy features, enormous limbs, tangled beard, grizzled hair, ashen white face, blue-tinged lips

properties.context: John Turner's only daughter; in love with James McCarthy; retained Lestrade to investigate in James's interest; visited Holmes at Hereford Arms
properties.physical_description: lovely young woman with violet eyes, pink flush on cheeks

properties.context: Scotland Yard detective retained by Alice Turner; referred the case to Holmes; sceptical of Holmes's deductions
properties.physical_description: lean, ferret-like man, furtive and sly-looking

properties.context: Game-keeper employed by Mr. Turner; witnessed Charles McCarthy walking alone towards Boscombe Pool and saw James McCarthy following minutes later
Girl of fourteen; daughter of the lodge-keeper of the Boscombe Valley estate; witnessed the quarrel between Charles and James McCarthy near Boscombe Pool
Lodge-keeper of Boscombe Valley estate and father of Patience Moran; Holmes wrote a note to him during investigation
Groom at Hatherley Farm who drove Charles McCarthy to Ross on the morning of the murder
John Turner's doctor; states Turner is a wreck with a shattered nervous system
Medical colleague of Watson's; referenced as someone who could cover Watson's patients during his absence
Barmaid in Bristol whom James McCarthy secretly married at a registry office; later revealed she already had a husband in the Bermuda Dockyard
Six-member highway robbery gang including Turner (Black Jack of Ballarat) and McCarthy; operated in Australian goldfields in the 1860s
properties.status: disbanded; three killed during Ballarat gold convoy attack

properties.context: Country district in Herefordshire; setting of the murder mystery; near Ross
Small lake at boundary between Hatherley Farm and Turner's estate; site of Charles McCarthy's murder
Farm let to Charles McCarthy by John Turner rent-free; starting point of McCarthy's final walk to Boscombe Pool
Pretty little country town in Herefordshire; where Holmes and Watson arrived; magistrates' court location
Hotel in Ross where Holmes and Watson stayed during the investigation
John Turner's estate and residence; adjacent to Hatherley Farm and Boscombe Pool
London railway station where Holmes and Watson departed for the west of England, Railway station near Watson's home; where Hatherley arrived; where Watson gets patients from officials
City where James McCarthy had spent three days with his secret barmaid wife; where Holmes wired for the map of Victoria
Gold mining town in Victoria, Australia; decoded from McCarthy's dying words 'ARAT'; home of the Ballarat Gang; where gold convoy was attacked
Australian colony where Turner and McCarthy knew each other; where the gold mines were; where Turner committed highway robbery as Black Jack of Ballarat
Location of the barmaid's existing husband, revealed when she abandoned James McCarthy
Charles McCarthy was struck from behind with a jagged stone by John Turner at Boscombe Pool on June 3rd (Monday); Turner fled leaving his grey cloak which he later retrieved

properties.description: Arrest and Acquittal of James McCarthy
properties.context: James McCarthy was arrested after inquest; verdict of wilful murder on Tuesday; brought before magistrates at Ross on Wednesday; acquitted at Assizes on strength of Holmes's objections
Turner's Ballarat Gang attacked a gold convoy from Ballarat to Melbourne in the early 1860s; McCarthy was the wagon driver; three gang members and four troopers were killed
John Turner confessed to Holmes and Watson; confession written down by Holmes, signed by Turner, witnessed by Watson; to be used only if McCarthy was condemned
Jagged stone found near the great beech tree at Boscombe Pool; identified by Holmes as the murder weapon from its correspondence with the injuries
Turner's grey cloak dropped in his flight after the murder; seen vaguely by James McCarthy; retrieved by Turner
Cigar ash and stump found behind the great beech tree at Boscombe Pool; identified by Holmes as an Indian cigar rolled in Rotterdam; used to identify the killer

properties.description: Map of the Colony of Victoria
properties.context: Wired for by Holmes from Bristol; used to decode 'ARAT' as 'BALLARAT' from McCarthy's dying words
Date of Charles McCarthy's murder at Boscombe Pool
Hydraulic engineer; client of Holmes; had his thumb severed by Colonel Lysander Stark's cleaver after discovering the counterfeiting operation; lost in business for two years before this case
properties.background: apprenticed to Venner & Matheson of Greenwich for seven years

properties.context: Thin German man leading the counterfeiting gang; hired Hatherley under false pretences; locked him in the hydraulic press; severed his thumb with a cleaver; fled with the gang before police arrived
properties.physical_description: exceeding thinness, face sharpened to nose and chin, skin tense over bones, German accent, grey eyes, cadaverous face

properties.context: Pretty woman living at the house; warned Hatherley to leave before it was too late; saved his life by opening the secret panel; called 'Elise' by Stark; status: fugitive; never found
properties.physical_description: pretty, dark dress of rich material, eager and beautiful face, frightened eyes

properties.context: Colonel Stark's secretary and manager; short thick man with a chinchilla beard; English; morose and silent; status: fugitive; never found
properties.physical_description: short thick man, chinchilla beard growing out of creases of double chin

properties.context: Englishman whose house name was given at Eyford Station; well-lined waistcoat; the house was actually used by the counterfeiting gang (Colonel Stark being his supposed 'patient')
Previous hydraulic engineer hired by Colonel Stark; went missing one year before the story after leaving lodgings at 10 PM; presumed murdered
Railway guard at Paddington who was a patient of Watson's; escorted Hatherley to Watson's consulting room
Criminal gang consisting of Colonel Stark, Ferguson, and Elise; used a hydraulic press to produce counterfeit coins (half-crowns using nickel/tin amalgam)
Well-known hydraulic engineering firm in Greenwich; where Hatherley served his seven-year apprenticeship
Small village in Berkshire near the borders of Oxfordshire, within seven miles of Reading; the railway station where Hatherley arrived; the destination for the investigation
Large whitewashed building approximately six miles from Eyford; contained the hydraulic press used for counterfeiting; caught fire from Hatherley's crushed oil-lamp; burned to the ground
Location of Hatherley's professional chambers (16A, 3rd floor)
Town within seven miles of Eyford; where Hatherley had to change trains; as far as Scotland Yard had traced the counterfeiting gang
Colonel Stark struck Hatherley's hand with a cleaver-like weapon as he hung from the window ledge trying to escape; Hatherley fell into the garden and later discovered his thumb had been cut off
The house caught fire from Hatherley's oil-lamp being crushed in the hydraulic press; the building burned to the ground; the criminals escaped with bulky boxes of coins
Gigantic hydraulic stamping machine used by the counterfeiting gang to press amalgam of nickel and tin into counterfeit coins; Hatherley was locked inside and nearly crushed; destroyed in the fire

Client of Holmes; large, short-sighted young woman and typist; deceived into falling in love with Hosmer Angel, who was her stepfather James Windibank in disguise; sought Holmes's help after Angel's disappearance
properties.physical_description: large woman, broad good-humoured face, short sight, wears pince-nez, double typewriting line on wrists, odd boots hastily put on
properties.income: £100/year from New Zealand stock, plus typewriting earnings

properties.context: Mary Sutherland's stepfather; wine merchant; disguised himself as Hosmer Angel to keep Mary emotionally bound and prevent her from marrying and losing her income; exposed by Holmes through typewriter analysis
properties.physical_description: sturdy, middle-sized, around 30 years old, clean-shaven, sallow-skinned, sharp penetrating grey eyes
properties.occupation: wine merchant / traveller for Westhouse & Marbank
properties.status: escaped when Holmes threatened him with the hunting crop

properties.context: Fictional persona created by James Windibank; alleged cashier in Leadenhall Street office; disappeared from a four-wheeler cab on the morning of his planned wedding to Mary Sutherland
properties.physical_description: five ft seven in height, strongly built, sallow complexion, black hair, slightly bald centre, bushy black side-whiskers, moustache, tinted glasses, slight speech infirmity
properties.occupation: alleged cashier at Leadenhall Street office

properties.context: Mary Sutherland's mother; married James Windibank shortly after her first husband's death; complicit in the Hosmer Angel deception
Former foreman of Mary Sutherland's father's plumbing business on Tottenham Court Road; helped carry on the business after her father's death; accompanied Mary and her mother to the gasfitters' ball
Referenced by Mary Sutherland as a person whose husband Holmes had found when police had given him up for dead; how Mary heard of Holmes
Mary Sutherland's uncle in Auckland who left her £2,500 in New Zealand stock generating her £100/year income
Referenced as having given Holmes a gold snuffbox with an amethyst as a souvenir for his assistance in the Irene Adler papers case
Persian poet quoted by Holmes at the end: 'There is danger for him who taketh the tiger cub'
Great claret importers of Fenchurch Street; employer of James Windibank as a wine traveller; confirmed Windibank's identity to Holmes
Referenced as having given Holmes a brilliant ring in return for services of great delicacy
Address of Mary Sutherland and her family
Street where Hosmer Angel's alleged cashier office was located; letters addressed to the post office there
Location of Westhouse & Marbank claret importers; employer of James Windibank
Church near King's Cross where the planned wedding of Mary Sutherland and Hosmer Angel was to take place
Hotel where the wedding breakfast was to be held after the planned marriage of Mary Sutherland and Hosmer Angel
City in France where Westhouse & Marbank had their French offices; Mary wrote to Windibank there before the wedding
Location of Uncle Ned who left Mary Sutherland £2,500 in New Zealand stock
Social event where Mary Sutherland first met Hosmer Angel (James Windibank in disguise); Mary's stepfather opposed her going

properties.description: Planned Wedding of Mary Sutherland and Hosmer Angel
properties.context: Planned for a Friday at St. Saviour's near King's Cross; Hosmer Angel (Windibank) disappeared from a four-wheeler cab before reaching the church
properties.venue: St. Saviour's Church near King's Cross

properties.context: Souvenir given to Holmes by the King of Bohemia as thanks for his assistance in the Irene Adler papers case
properties.material: old gold with great amethyst in centre of lid

properties.context: Remarkable ring given to Holmes by the reigning family of Holland in return for services of great delicacy
All typewritten including signature; provided to Holmes by Mary Sutherland; used by Holmes to match typewriter defects to Windibank's machine
Machine used by James Windibank at his office; used to type letters as Hosmer Angel; identified by Holmes through characteristic defects in the 'e' and 'r' keys
Advertisement placed by Mary Sutherland in the Saturday Chronicle describing Hosmer Angel
